original_reasoning: الدعرمة=قصر الخطو في عجلة (short hurried steps). τελεοδρομέω=to complete the course/race. Both involve locomotion along a path. Short hurried steps vs completing a race share the movement-along-path concept, though scale differs.
Both sides are plant names, so a botanical transfer is plausible even without a direct gloss match.

أصتت الأرض: لم يكن فيها بقل ولا كلأ (the land had no vegetation or pasture - barren/still). ἕσταμαι=standing, standing still. Barren land where nothing grows IS land that stands still/stagnant. The concept of motionless sterility connects them.
Arabic ghadar relates to ghadir/ghader; Greek = Gindarus (Syria), a Semitic place name. Shared Semitic substrate plausible.
Arabic belly-slackening can imply inner lack or want, which plausibly meets Greek crave or need.
Arabic gharasa = plant; Greek Gerasa = Jerash in Jordan, Semitic place name. Possible substrate match through place naming.
Both sides sit in the onomastic-Persian/royal sphere, giving a plausible direct-name linkage.
Arabic includes cutting with an instrument, and mortar is likewise a hard working tool; the functional core is plausible.
